coredata.dc:title: Biocompatible fluorescent organic nanoparticles derived from glucose and polyethylenimine
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xiqi
Zhang, Xiaoyong
Yang, Bin
Yang, Yang
Chen, Qiaomei
Wei, Yen
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent organic nanoparticles (FONs) were facilely prepared from polyethylenimine and glucose at mild reaction condition, and further utilized for cell imaging with various fluorescent wavelengths. The as-prepared PEI-Glu FONs were fully characterized by a series of techniques including 1H NMR spectrum, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, dynamic light scattering, UV–vis absorption spectrum, and fluorescence spectra. Such FONs were demonstrated with intense fluorescence and high water dispersibility. Biocompatibility evaluation and cell uptake behavior of these FONs were further investigated, which proved excellent biocompatibility and made them promising for cell imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyethylenimine
Glucose
Fluorescent organic nanoparticles
Biocompatibility
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Confocal microscopic imaging of cells incubated with PEI-Glu FONs
Cell imaging applications of PEI-Glu FONs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XPS spectra of PEI-Glu FONs: (A) raw XPS data showing the presence of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen; (B) C 1s spectrum with peak at ∼285eV for sp2 carbon atoms and shoulder peak at ∼287eV for sp3 carbon atoms; (C) N 1s spectrum with peak at ∼398eV. (D) O 1s spectrum with peak at ∼531eV.

(A) FT-IR spectra of PEI, Glucose, and PEI-Glu; (B) TEM image of PEI-Glu FONs dispersed in water, scale bar=100nm; (C) UV absorption spectrum of PEI-Glu FONs dispersed in water, inset is the visible image of PEI-Glu FONs in water; (D) PL emission spectra (right) of PEI-Glu FONs dispersed in water under different excitation wavelengths and the excitation spectrum (left), inset is the fluorescent images of PEI-Glu FONs taken under UV light of 365nm.

Biocompatibility evaluations of PEI-Glu FONs. (A–C) Optical microscopy images of A549 cells incubated with different concentrations of PEI-Glu FONs for 24h: (A) control cells, (B) 10μgmL−1, (C) 80μgmL−1; (D) cell viability of PEI-Glu FONs for 8h and 24h.
CLSM images of A549 cells incubated with 10μgmL−1 of PEI-Glu FONs for 3h. (A) Bright field, (B) excited with 405nm laser, (C) excited with 458nm laser, (D) merged image of A and B. Scale bar=20μm.
Preparation of PEI-Glu FONs from PEI and glucose for cell imaging applications.

head.ce:title: Biocompatible fluorescent organic nanoparticles derived from glucose and polyethylenimine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and the Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and the Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research, Tsinghua University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry/Institute of Polymers, Nanchang University, 999 Xuefu Avenue, Nanchang 330031, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry/Institute of Polymers, Nanchang University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent organic nanoparticles (FONs) were facilely prepared from polyethylenimine and glucose at mild reaction condition, and further utilized for cell imaging with various fluorescent wavelengths. The as-prepared PEI-Glu FONs were fully characterized by a series of techniques including 1H NMR spectrum, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, dynamic light scattering, UV–vis absorption spectrum, and fluorescence spectra. Such FONs were demonstrated with intense fluorescence and high water dispersibility. Biocompatibility evaluation and cell uptake behavior of these FONs were further investigated, which proved excellent biocompatibility and made them promising for cell imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Cellular fluorescence imaging has been regarded as a versatile visualization way for medical diagnosis and clinical study, and becomes a useful tool to monitor various biological processes within the sophisticated intracellular systems with high sensitive and noninvasive superiority [1–3]. So far, various fluorescent bioprobes have been reported and extensively studied for bioimaging applications over the past few decades [4–11]. For example, green fluorescent protein (GFP) is commonly used as a reporter of expression, and can be introduced into organisms, and maintains in its genome through breeding, injection, or cell transformation. However, GFP often suffers from poor photostability, small stokes shifts, easy enzyme degradation, and tedious transfection process [12]. Rare earth-doped nanoparticles and semiconductor quantum dots are other two important types of fluorescent bioprobes, which are endowed with high luminescent intensity and excellent resistance to photobleaching [13–16]. But their high cytotoxicity derived from the heavy metal ions and susceptibility to aggregate in cellular environments greatly hamper them for practical applications [17].

Recently, fluorescent organic nanoparticles (FONs) have received increasing attentions for cellular fluorescence imaging due to their biodegradation potential, ease of functionalization, and flexible synthetic approaches of organic dyes [18–21]. However, as a result of the poor miscibility in water, organic dyes are prone to aggregate in aqueous media, which limits their intracorporal applications. In this case, ionic luminophores have been employed for cell imaging in consequence of their excellent water miscibilities. However, their inherent charges will perturb membrane potential and even kill the cells at high concentration [22]. Moreover, most of the conventional organic dyes are hydrophobic planar structures with strong intermolecular π–π interactions, which will inevitably lead to notorious aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching problem at aggregated phase [23]. In addition, low resistance to photobleaching is another shortcoming for most organic dyes, which also hampers their application as bioprobes [24]. Accordingly, aggregation induced emission (AIE) materials with antiquenching effect, which can emit intense fluorescence in the aggregated state, have emerged and rapidly aroused great interest of many researchers [25]. Hereafter, a wide spectrum of molecular architectures of AIE compounds have been developed for potential chemosensors and bioimaging applications [26–35]. Meanwhile, various strategies of constructing AIE based FONs, including non-covalent and covalent methods, have also been reported [36–47]. Despite many impressive advances in AIE based FONs, more facile preparation method without complex synthesis and expensive reactant is still highly demanded.

Polyethylenimine (PEI) is a water soluble polymer with repeating unit composed of the amine group and two carbon aliphatic ethyl spacers. Particularly, branched PEIs contain highly reactive primary and secondary amino groups, which can react with epoxy, aldehyde, and isocyanate compound, and could find many applications in epoxy resin reaction agent, formaldehyde adsorbent, and dye fixing agent. PEI is still a polymeric transfection agent and extremely appealing for building siRNA carriers [48–50]. In addition, glucose is produced commercially through the enzymatic hydrolysis of starch, and many crops can be used as the source of starch. Glucose is also an important carbohydrate in biology as a secondary source of energy and a metabolic intermediate for cells [51].

In this contribution, a novel strategy of preparing FONs was developed for the first time via incorporating branched PEI and glucose at mild reaction condition (Scheme 1 ). For the synthesis of PEI-Glu FONs, PEI and glucose were dissolved in aqueous solution, while the reaction was carried out at 100°C for 2h to afford the FONs. After purification by simple dialysis, the obtain FONs with high water dispersibility could be used directly for cell imaging. The mechanism of the FONs formation might involve Schiff base condensation between PEI and glucose, which would be discussed in the main text.

Polyethyleneimine (PEI, M
n
=600, Aladdin Industrial Corporation), glucose (Aladdin Industrial Corporation) were used as received. All other solvents and chemicals were purchased from commercial sources and used directly without further purification.

1H NMR spectrum was measured on a JEOL 400MHz spectrometer [D2O as solvent and tetramethylsilane (TMS) as the internal standard]. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were performed on a VGESCALAB 220-IXL spectrometer using an Al Ka X-ray source (1486.6eV). The energy scale was internally calibrated by referencing to the binding energy (Eb) of the C1s peak of a carbon contaminant at 284.6eV. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were obtained in a transmission mode on a Shimadzu Spectrum 8400 spectrometer (Japan). Typically, 8 scans at a resolution of 1cm−1 were accumulated to obtain one spectrum. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was recorded on a HT7700 microscope (Hitachi, Japan) operated at 100kV, the TEM specimen was made by placing a drop of the nanoparticles suspension on a carbon-coated copper grid. The size distribution of PEI-Glu FONs in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was determined using a zeta Plus apparatus (ZetaPlus, Brookhaven Instruments, Holtsville, NY). UV–vis absorption spectrum was recorded on UV/vis/NIR 2600 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) using quartz cuvettes of 1cm path length. Fluorescence spectra were measured on an F-4600 spectrometer with a slit width of 3nm for both excitation and emission.

PEI (100mg) and glucose (200mg) were dispersed in 20mL deionized water for 2h at 100°C. Then the mixture was purified by dialysis through porous cellulose bag (molecular weight cut off 3500Da) using ethanol. Finally the product inside the dialysis bag was collected and dried by vacuum oven at 40°C.

Cell morphology was observed to examine the effects of PEI-Glu FONs to A549 cells (adenocarcinomic human alveolar basal epithelial cells, origin from American Type Culture Collection). Briefly, cells were seeded in 6-well microplates at a density of 1×105 cellsmL−1 in 2mL of respective media containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS). After cell attachment, plates were washed with PBS and cells were treated with complete cell culture medium, or different concentrations of PEI-Glu FONs prepared in 10% FBS containing media for 24h. Then all samples were washed with PBS three times to remove the uninternalized nanoparticles. The morphology of cells was observed by using an optical microscopy (Leica, Germany), the overall magnification was 100×.

The cell viability of PEI-Glu FONs on A549 cells was evaluated by cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay. Briefly, cells were seeded in 96-well microplates at a density of 5×104 cellsmL−1 in 160μL of respective media containing 10% FBS. After 24h of cell attachment, the cells were incubated with 10, 20, 40, 80, 120μgmL−1 PEI-Glu FONs for 8 and 24h. Then nanoparticles were removed and cells were washed with PBS three times. 10μL of CCK-8 dye and 100μL of DMEM cell culture medium were added to each well and incubated for 2h at 37°C. Plates were then analyzed with a microplate reader (VictorШ, Perkin-Elmer). Measurements of formazan dye absorbance were carried out at 450nm, with the reference wavelength at 620nm. The values were proportional to the number of live cells. The percent reduction of CCK-8 dye was compared to controls (cells not exposure to PEI-Glu FONs), which represented 100% CCK-8 reduction. Three replicate wells were used per microplate, and the experiment was repeated three times. Cell survival was expressed as absorbance relative to that of untreated controls. Results are presented as mean±standard deviation (SD).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confocal microscopic imaging of cells incubated with PEI-Glu FONs
ce:section.ce:para: A549 cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% heat-inactivated FBS, 2mM glutamine, 100UmL−1 penicillin, and 100μgmL−1 of streptomycin. Cell culture was maintained at 37°C in a humidified condition of 95% air and 5% CO2 in culture medium. Culture medium was changed every three days for maintaining the exponential growth of the cells. On the day prior to treatment, cells were seeded in a glass bottom dish with a density of 1×105 cells per dish. On the day of treatment, the cells were incubated with PEI-Glu FONs at a final concentration of 10μgmL−1 for 3h at 37°C. Afterward, the cells were washed three times with PBS to remove the PEI-Glu FONs and then fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 10min at room temperature. Cell images were taken with a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) Zesis 710 3-channel (Zesis, Germany) with the excitation wavelength of 405nm.

For the characterization of the morphology and fluorescent properties of the PEI-Glu FONs, a variety of techniques, including 1H NMR spectrum, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS), UV–vis absorption spectrum, and fluorescence spectra were carried out to determine the performances of PEI-Glu FONs.

The 1H NMR spectrum of PEI-Glu FONs in D2O was conducted and shown in Fig. S1. The chemical shifts of PEI (2.0–3.0ppm) and glucose (3.0–4.0ppm) could be found in the spectrum of these FONs, indicating the successful combination of PEI and glucose to afford PEI-Glu FONs. To further characterize the chemical information about the as-prepared PEI-Glu FONs, elemental composition of these FONs has been determined by XPS studies, which is shown in Fig. 1 . The XPS result showed the presence of carbon as major component along with other two minor components of nitrogen and oxygen, respectively. The overall wt% of elements present in FONs was calculated as C:N:O ∼63:19:18. The raw XPS data of the FONs showed three signals corresponding to carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen (Fig. 1A). The C 1s XPS spectrum could be deconvoluted into 2 components (Fig. 1B). The main peak at ∼285eV and shoulder peak ∼287eV could be attributed to carbon with sp2 and sp3 carbon atoms, respectively, indicating that Schiff base condensation between PEI and glucose might occur to afford CN in the PEI-Glu FONs. The N 1s spectrum showed peak centered at ∼398eV (Fig. 1C), while the O 1s spectrum showed peaks at ∼ 531eV (Fig. 1D). All of the above results confirmed that PEI and glucose were successfully incorporated into the PEI-Glu FONs.

FT-IR spectra were further determined to confirm the successful synthesis of PEI-Glu FONs. As shown in Fig. 2A, the characteristic peak of NH2 located at 3275cm−1 was observed in the sample of PEI, meanwhile, another two peaks located at 2936 and 2817cm−1 could be ascribed to the stretching vibrations of the CH2 groups. Moreover, the bending vibration of NH2 located at 1577cm−1 and stretching vibration of the CN band located at 1060cm−1 were also found in PEI. In the case of glucose, the broad peak of OH located at 3268cm−1 could be ascribed to the stretching vibration of the OH group, while strong stretching vibration of the CO band was found at 1016cm−1. After reaction of PEI and glucose at 100°C for 2h to afford the PEI-Glu FONs, some characteristic peaks could be clearly observed. The stretching vibrations of NH2 and OH groups were found at 3268cm−1, and another stretching vibration of the CH2 group located at 2960 and 2824cm−1 also could be observed with decreased intensity compared with that of PEI. Strong stretching vibrations of the CO band found at 1016cm−1 and the CN band located at 1093cm−1 also emerged in the FONs. More importantly, the stretching vibration of the CN band located at 1624cm−1 and bending vibration of CH group located at 792cm−1 could be clearly seen, demonstrating that Schiff base condensation occurred between PEI and glucose. Due to the existence of many hydrophilic functional groups such as NH2 and OH on the surface of the FONs, it is expected to show excellent dispersibility in aqueous solution.

The TEM experiment was carried out to confirm the morphology of the conjugated PEI-Glu FONs (Fig. 2B). Many spherical nanoparticles with diameters ranging from 10 to 30nm could be clearly identified. The TEM images shown here gave us direct evidence of the formation of nanoparticles in aqueous solution. Meanwhile, the size distribution of PEI-Glu FONs in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was determined using a zeta Plus particle size analyzer, which indicated that the size distribution was 101.7±7.1nm, with a polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.245. As compared with the size distribution in PBS, the size characterized by TEM was somewhat smaller, which might be due to the drying-causing shrinkage of the FONs. The hydrolytic stability of PEI-Glu FONs in PBS has been studied by DLS in a period of 10 days, which showed the value of 95.1±5.9nm with slight change, demonstrating good hydrolytic stability of these FONs.

The UV absorption spectrum of PEI-Glu FONs dispersed in water was shown in Fig. 2C, it could be noticed that the absorption peak was located at 326nm as a shoulder peak. It is noteworthy that the entire spectrum started to increase from 600nm, which is ascribed to the Mie effect, indicating the existence of nanoparticles in the solution [52,53]. The inset of Fig. 2C proved that PEI-Glu FONs were readily dispersed in water with high transparency. The PL spectra of PEI-Glu FONs in water were also determined and shown in Fig. 2D. The FONs showed obvious excitation dependent emission spectra, which was found that emission maxima red-shifted with the increment of the excitation wavelength. When the excitation wavelength increased from 320nm to 500nm, the maximum emission wavelength accordingly increased from 468nm to 550nm, which covered the wavelength ranging from blue to green. Moreover, the excitation-dependent fluorescent properties of FONs have also been demonstrated by some other types of fluorescent nanoparticles [21,54], however, the underline mechanism is still unclear. The inset of Fig. 2D represented the fluorescent image of PEI-Glu FONs taken under UV light of 365nm, where bright sky blue fluorescence could be found. The fluorescence quantum yield of these FONs was calculated as high as 15% using 9,10-diphenylanthracene as the reference. This obtained bright fluorescence is believed greatly beneficial to the potential cell imaging applications.

The pH stability of PEI-Glu FONs has been investigated and shown in Fig. S2. The pH stability of the as-prepared FONs was studied in a period of 10 days, which showed a slight change in the emission intensity, indicating excellent pH stability of the FONs. It is interesting to find that the fluorescent intensity of FONs showed obvious enhancement at acidic condition with pH value of 2, however, the fluorescent intensity decreased significantly at alkaline condition with pH value of 12. Although the chemical structures of the as-prepared compounds are not clear, the creation of inherently fluorescent centers from hyperbranched PEI can be modulated by adjusting the acidity of the compounds [55].

The biocompatibility was carried out to evaluate the potential biomedical applications of PEI-Glu FONs. The influences of PEI-Glu FONs to A549 cells (adenocarcinomic human alveolar basal epithelial cells) were firstly examined by optical microscopy after the cells were incubated with different concentrations of PEI-Glu FONs for 24h (Figs. 3A–C). The result showed that the cells grew well when incubated with 10 and 80μgmL−1 of PEI-Glu FONs, suggesting that the FONs were highly biocompatible with cells. To further confirm the cytocompatibility of PEI-Glu FONs, cell viability of PEI-Glu FONs to A549 cells was determined by cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay. As shown in Fig. 3D, no decrease of cell viability was found when the cells were incubated with 10–120μgmL−1 of PEI-Glu FONs for 8h and 24h. Even when the concentration was up to 120μgmL−1, the cell viability value was still greater than 90%, suggesting great potential of FONs for biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell imaging applications of PEI-Glu FONs
ce:section.ce:para: Cell imaging applications of PEI-Glu FONs were further explored. The cell uptake behavior of PEI-Glu FONs was evaluated by Confocal Laser Scanning Microscope (CLSM) observation. As shown in Fig. 4B, when excited with laser of 405nm, strong blue fluorescence could be observed at the cell location after they were incubated with only 10μgmL−1 of PEI-Glu FONs. When the excitation wavelength of the laser was changed to 458nm, bright green fluorescence could be seen within the cell at the same incubated condition due to the excitation dependent emission behavior (Fig. 4C). Furthermore, many areas with relative weak fluorescence intensity were found in the cells, which might be the location of cell nucleus (Figs. 4B and C), indicating PEI-Glu FONs could be facilely uptaken by cells and mainly located at cytoplasm. In addition, these FONs were considered uptaken by endocytosis of the cells. Thanks to the intense fluorescence derived from the PEI-Glu FONs, strong signal could be detected, which was greatly beneficial for cell imaging.

In summary, a novel strategy of preparing FONs was developed for the first time via incorporating branched PEI and glucose at mild reaction condition. The reaction was carried out at 100°C for 2h to afford PEI-Glu FONs. Such FONs showed high water dispersibility with diameters ranging from 10 to 30nm according to the TEM result. The XPS and FT-IR spectra have demonstrated the mechanism of the formation of PEI-Glu FONs might involve Schiff base condensation between PEI and glucose. The PEI-Glu FONs showed bright fluorescence from blue to green in aqueous solution with excitation dependent emission behavior. Biocompatibility evaluation and cell imaging results suggested that these FONs were highly biocompatible for bioimaging applications. More importantly, the preparation strategy of biocompatible FONs reported in this work can extend to various carbohydrates, biomolecules, and other water soluble polymers with amino groups, which may provide a new path to prepare more novel fluorescent materials for biomedical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21134004, 21201108, 51363016), and the National 973 Project (No. 2011CB935700), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2012M520243, 2013T60100).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2014.10.027.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:
